theme: Prioritisation frameworks — RICE, ICE, and MoSCoW
objective: Score 8 PhonePe features using the RICE formula with full arithmetic working shown for each, rank all 8, select the top 3 for a pre-Diwali launch with justification, and give one-sentence business reasoning for dropping each of the remaining 5.

realWorldAnchor.example: Before a Diwali or IPL season launch, a Swiggy PM runs through the backlog using a prioritisation framework to decide which features get engineering time — festive seasons represent disproportionate revenue and user acquisition in India, meaning the Reach and Confidence inputs in RICE shift significantly for features that serve high-volume moments.

teamContext: Prioritisation decisions directly involve Engineering (who estimates Effort in person-months) and Marketing/Growth (who provides Reach estimates and impact context for campaign-linked features) — the PM's job is to synthesize inputs from both teams into a scored framework, not to decide unilaterally.

tasks.title: RICE: Simple Prioritization for Product Managers — Intercom
tasks.summary: This is the original blog post from Intercom that introduced and defined the RICE scoring model — Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort. Intercom's PM team created RICE to solve a specific internal problem: too many competing priorities and no shared scoring language across teams. The article explains the formula precisely, defines each variable with examples, and shows worked calculations. This is the definitive primary source for the framework.
keyTakeaways: RICE Score = (Reach × Impact × Confidence%) divided by Effort in person-months — the formula must be applied consistently across all features being compared, otherwise the resulting scores cannot be ranked against each other

Confidence is the most underused and most important variable: it forces honesty about how well the team actually knows the Reach and Impact estimates, preventing overconfident bets on features that are exciting but untested

RICE works best for comparing features within the same product area and timeframe — it breaks down when comparing a speculative 0-to-1 feature (low confidence, high potential) against an incremental improvement (high confidence, modest impact), because those are fundamentally different types of bets

tasks.title: How to Prioritize Product Features — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky surveys how top product teams at companies like Airbnb, Figma, and Duolingo actually prioritise features — not just what framework they use, but how they balance quantitative scoring with strategic judgment and when they override the numbers. The article is especially useful for understanding the limits of RICE and the most common prioritisation mistakes.
keyTakeaways: No framework replaces strategic judgment: a feature with a high RICE score that contradicts the company's current strategic focus should still be deprioritised — frameworks inform decisions, they do not make them

The three most common RICE mistakes: treating effort estimates from engineers as precise (they are wide estimates), forgetting that Reach estimates decay as the product matures, and conflating impact on users with impact on the specific business metric you are trying to move this quarter

MoSCoW (Must Have / Should Have / Could Have / Won't Have) is most useful for scoping a specific release, not for cross-feature portfolio prioritisation — use RICE to decide what to build across the quarter, use MoSCoW to decide what ships in the next sprint

tasks.keyword: RICE prioritization worked example product manager feature scoring table India startup walkthrough
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing a PM walk through RICE on a realistic feature list — with actual numbers in a table, arithmetic shown, and final rankings justified — is where the framework becomes something you can use rather than just describe. Look for blog posts or PM prep resources that show a full scoring table with working, not just the formula definition.
whatToLearn: How experienced PMs estimate Reach when exact data is not available — the acceptable approximation methods (monthly active users × estimated feature touch rate), and how to be explicit about assumptions so they can be challenged

What a final RICE output looks like structurally: a table with all features ranked by score, followed by a short narrative justifying the top choices and explaining why high-scoring features were still dropped for strategic reasons

tasks.promptTitle: RICE score 8 PhonePe features — live coaching with a skeptical PM lead
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM lead coaching me through a RICE scoring exercise for 8 PhonePe features.

Today I studied:
- RICE framework: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort in person-months
- When to override RICE with strategic judgment and why
- MoSCoW as a release scoping tool (distinct from RICE as a portfolio prioritisation tool)

Real world context: I am an APM at PhonePe. It is September, eight weeks before Diwali. Engineering has capacity for approximately 3 features before the festive season. I need to present a prioritised shortlist to the PM lead today.

Your role today: Play the skeptical PM lead. When I score each feature, question my Reach estimate ('where did that number come from?'), my Confidence ('are you really 80% confident — what evidence do you have?'), and my Effort estimate ('have you actually talked to engineering, or is this your guess?'). Do not let me get away with undefended numbers.

The 8 features with input data:

Feature 1: UPI payment via WhatsApp integration
Reach: 5,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 8 person-months

Feature 2: Credit score display on home dashboard
Reach: 8,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 90% | Effort: 2 person-months

Feature 3: Split bill feature for friend groups
Reach: 2,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 6 person-months

Feature 4: Voice commands in Hindi for payments
Reach: 12,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 50% | Effort: 12 person-months

Feature 5: Auto-pay setup wizard for utility bills
Reach: 6,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 4 person-months

Feature 6: Cashback gamification for regular users
Reach: 10,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 5 person-months

Feature 7: Offline NFC tap-to-pay
Reach: 3,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 40% | Effort: 14 person-months

Feature 8: Cross-border remittance to Nepal and Bangladesh
Reach: 1,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 55% | Effort: 10 person-months

Activity:
Step 1: I calculate the RICE score for each feature and show my working (full formula with numbers).
Step 2: I rank all 8 by RICE score.
Step 3: I pick the top 3 for pre-Diwali launch and justify, then explain why I am dropping the others.
You challenge my numbers at each step before I move on.

After all steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Hindi voice commands and offline NFC have very different strategic value in India's festive season context and in Tier 2/3 market expansion than their RICE scores alone suggest — the strategic context changes the real priority even if the numbers do not]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for you]

Begin with: 'Start with Feature 1: UPI via WhatsApp. Show me the formula and the number. Then tell me — where exactly did the Reach estimate of 5 million come from?'

bonusTasks.title: MoSCoW prioritisation framework explained — Product School
bonusTasks.summary: This Product School article explains MoSCoW (Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, Won't Have) using a realistic launch scenario. Reading this after RICE sharpens the mental model of which framework to use when: RICE for cross-feature portfolio decisions across a quarter, MoSCoW for scoping what ships in a specific release. Choosing the right framework for the right context is itself a signal of PM maturity in interviews.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Score all 8 PhonePe features using the RICE formula with full arithmetic working shown for each, rank them 1-8, select the top 3 for pre-Diwali launch with 2-sentence justification each, and drop the bottom 5 with 1-sentence business reasoning each.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at PhonePe. It is September — 8 weeks before Diwali. Engineering has capacity for approximately 3 features. Score these 8 features using RICE (formula: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort in person-months):

Feature 1: UPI via WhatsApp integration — Reach: 5,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 8 person-months
Feature 2: Credit score on home dashboard — Reach: 8,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 90% | Effort: 2 person-months
Feature 3: Split bill for friend groups — Reach: 2,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 6 person-months
Feature 4: Hindi voice commands for payments — Reach: 12,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 50% | Effort: 12 person-months
Feature 5: Auto-pay utility bills wizard — Reach: 6,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 4 person-months
Feature 6: Cashback gamification for regulars — Reach: 10,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 5 person-months
Feature 7: Offline NFC tap-to-pay — Reach: 3,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 40% | Effort: 14 person-months
Feature 8: Cross-border remittance Nepal/Bangladesh — Reach: 1,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 55% | Effort: 10 person-months
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part 1: A table with columns: Feature | Reach | Impact | Confidence | Effort | RICE Score | Working (show the arithmetic). Part 2: All 8 features ranked highest to lowest RICE score. Part 3: Top 3 selected with a 2-sentence justification each (why this feature earns its spot in the pre-Diwali launch). Part 4: Bottom 5 dropped with a 1-sentence business reasoning for each drop — not just 'low RICE score' but a strategic reason.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 5: Prioritisation Frameworks — RICE, ICE, and MoSCoW.

I studied today:
- RICE framework: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort — each variable defined precisely with the Intercom source
- When to override RICE with strategic judgment, and the three most common RICE scoring mistakes
- MoSCoW as a release scoping tool, distinct from RICE as a portfolio prioritisation tool

Real world context I should apply:
PhonePe APM, September, 8 weeks before Diwali. Engineering capacity for 3 features. Festive season context changes the value of certain features — reach estimates spike, confidence in engagement metrics is higher during festive windows, and time-pressure makes effort estimates critical.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: RICE score 8 PhonePe features, rank them, select top 3 with justification, drop bottom 5 with business reasoning.

Here is my work:
[Paste your RICE table, ranking, top 3 justification, and drop reasons here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — check arithmetic for each feature's score, and whether the top 3 make strategic sense given the Diwali context]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., strategic fit with Diwali gifting and payments behaviour, the fact that UPI volume spikes 3-4x during festive season, or competitor moves that change the value of specific features]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about how festive season context in India — Diwali, IPL, Big Billion Day — creates a product prioritisation dynamic unique to Indian consumer internet: Reach estimates change, confidence from past festive data is more reliable, and features that look marginal in a normal month can become critical during a festive window]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
